data.user_message:
Hello! I'm glad you reached out. Let me introduce myself — I'm the Director of Employment Services at the Palestinian Ministry of Labor. My division is responsible for connecting young Palestinians, mostly between fifteen and twenty-nine, with real work opportunities across the West Bank and Gaza. We have regional offices, a small central team, and we work with partners like the Palestinian Employment Fund and Enabel. 
Right, so — what I'm hoping we can build is basically a central platform, a single place where young Palestinians looking for work can find real job opportunities, and where employers can actually find the right people without being flooded with unsuitable applicants. Right now everything is completely scattered — jobs get posted on different websites, passed around by word of mouth, written on paper notices — and my staff are spending all their time doing things by hand that should just... happen automatically. The people who would use this are young job seekers, employers of all kinds, my ministry staff, and honestly our regional office workers too. The main thing I want — and I cannot stress this enough — is that we stop losing track of people. A young person registers with us, we help them, and then we never know what happened. That has to change.
data.assistant_message: I understand you're looking to create a central platform for job seekers and employers. What are the three most important things a user must be able to do with this platform?

data.user_message: Honestly, I don't really think in those terms — I mean, I'm not a software person. But if you're asking what matters most to me... the first thing is that a young person who walks into any of our offices — or maybe even does it from home — should be able to put their information in once, their education, their skills, their experience, and have that information actually work for them. Not just sit in a file somewhere. The second thing is that employers should be able to post what they're looking for and actually get the right people showing up — not a flood of completely unsuitable applicants like they complain about now. And the third thing... I want my analysts to finally be able to answer basic questions like which sectors are hiring, what skills are in demand, where the jobs are. Right now we're just guessing, and that's embarrassing when our government partners and international donors ask us for numbers.

data.user_message: That's actually a really good question, and it's something I think about a lot. Right now, employers advertise everywhere — some use local websites like jobs.ps, some put up paper notices, some just call people they know personally. It's completely scattered. What I'd love is if somehow all of that could come together in one place — so that when a young person comes to us, we're not just showing them the three vacancies we happen to know about, we're showing them everything that's out there. I'm not sure how that works technically, but if these other job websites could somehow share their listings with us automatically, that would be incredible. Though I'll be honest — I do wonder whether those websites would actually agree to that. They're private businesses, after all.

data.user_message: Well, the basics obviously — what the job is, who the employer is, where it's located, what kind of work it involves. But what really matters to me is that the listing includes what skills or qualifications are actually needed, because that's the only way we can figure out whether a particular young person is a good fit or not. And honestly, the salary range matters too — a lot of our young people are in very difficult economic situations and they need to know upfront if a job is even worth pursuing. I guess things like whether it's full-time or part-time, and whether it's in Gaza or the West Bank, are important too — because our situation geographically is... complicated.
data.assistant_message:
<REQ type="functional" category="job_listing_details">
The system shall require job listings to include job title, employer name, location, required skills or qualifications, salary range, employment type (full-time or part-time), and region (Gaza or West Bank).
</REQ>
Got it. Now, regarding matching employers with the right candidates, how should the platform decide which applicants are suitable for a job?

data.user_message: Yes, this is really the heart of it, isn't it? I just want the right young person to appear in front of the right employer — automatically, without my staff having to guess or make ten phone calls. Right now it's completely manual — someone on my team looks at a CV and thinks "oh, maybe this person would suit that vacancy" based on gut feeling. That's not good enough. So I'd want the platform to look at what a young person has — their education, their skills, their experience — and compare it to what the employer is asking for, and then surface the best matches. But I'll be honest — when you say it will do this "automatically," I want to understand what that actually means in practice. How does it know who is a genuinely good fit? Because if it just matches keywords and misses the real picture, we haven't solved anything.
data.assistant_message: How should the platform evaluate and rank candidates to ensure it identifies the best matches for a job, beyond just matching keywords?

data.user_message: Honestly, I'm not sure I can answer that in technical terms — that's more your area than mine. But what I can tell you is what a good outcome looks like from where I sit. A good match isn't just someone who has the right certificate — it's someone whose skills actually fit the work, who is available, who is in the right location or willing to travel, and maybe who has shown commitment by completing training programmes we've run. I'd also want the system to give employers a small number of strong candidates rather than a long list of mediocre ones — because employers have told me directly that being flooded with unsuitable applicants is one of their biggest complaints. So whatever method you use behind the scenes, the result should feel like a thoughtful recommendation, not just a search result.

data.user_message: I'd say somewhere between five and ten — enough that the employer has real choice, but not so many that they're overwhelmed and just give up. Though honestly, I think what matters more than the exact number is the quality. If the platform can reliably give an employer five people who are genuinely suitable, that's worth a hundred random applications. Maybe the number could even vary depending on how many qualified people are registered — I wouldn't want to artificially limit it if there are fifteen excellent candidates. But the default should be small and strong, not large and weak.

data.user_message:
Well, the basics obviously need to be there — their name, their contact information, their education, their skills, their work experience if they have any. But I also want to capture things like whether they have a disability, because we have a responsibility to support inclusion, and some employers specifically want to hire people with disabilities. And I'd want to know where they're located — which governorate, which city — because that affects what opportunities are realistic for them.

As for how long to keep it... honestly, I hadn't thought about that. I suppose as long as they're actively looking for work? But I'd want them to be able to update their information themselves when things change — if they finish a new training course, or get a new qualification. The worst thing would be if their profile becomes outdated and starts working against them instead of for them.

data.user_message: I guess the system should probably remind them — send them a message saying "your profile hasn't been updated in a while, is everything still accurate?" Because the last thing I want is for a young person to miss a good opportunity because their profile is showing old information. But I wouldn't want to just delete their profile without warning — that would feel wrong, especially if they're in a difficult situation and just haven't had the chance to log in for a while. So maybe a reminder first, and then if there's still no response after some reasonable time, perhaps the profile becomes inactive rather than deleted completely.

data.user_message: Honestly, this feels like a detail I'd need to think about more carefully with my legal colleagues — there may be rules about how long government systems can hold personal information. But from a practical standpoint, I wouldn't want to lose data that could be useful for our labour market analysis. Even if a young person has found work and is no longer actively looking, knowing that they found work, what sector they went into, what their background was — that's valuable information for our analysts. So I'd separate the two things: the active profile that the job seeker uses day to day, and the anonymised statistical data that we keep for policy purposes. The personal details maybe have a natural lifespan, but the patterns and numbers should stay.

data.user_message: Oh, this is actually a real headache for us. Right now there's very little coordination — each regional office basically operates on its own. A young person registers in Ramallah, and the Hebron office has no idea they exist. If that person moves or is willing to travel, we're completely missing opportunities. My central team has no real visibility into what's happening across the regions — I can't tell you right now how many people registered in Nablus last month or what vacancies were filled in Jericho. Everything is local and disconnected. What I'd want is for the platform to work the same way everywhere — so that a job seeker registered anywhere in Palestine is visible to the right employer regardless of which office they walked into, and my central team can see the full picture at any time.

data.user_message: Look, I think the principle is simple even if the details aren't — anyone working within the Ministry should be able to see what they need to do their job properly, but not necessarily everything. A regional officer in Ramallah probably needs to see job seekers and vacancies in their area, and maybe neighbouring areas too. But my central analysts need to see the whole country — all regions, all sectors, all trends. And there should be some things that only senior people can access, like individual personal details, while the regional staff work more with the practical day-to-day matching. I guess what I'm saying is that different people in the Ministry need different levels of access depending on their role — but everyone should be working from the same shared information rather than separate paper files in separate offices.

data.user_message:
This is honestly the thing that frustrates me the most — and I mean that. Right now, once a young person leaves our office, they disappear. We have no idea what happens to them. Did they get an interview? Did they get hired? Did they take a training course and improve their skills? We have absolutely no way of knowing, and that is deeply embarrassing when our international partners like Enabel or the Palestinian Employment Fund ask us what outcomes we're achieving. We're spending public money and donor money on employment services and we cannot demonstrate a single successful placement with confidence.

So what I want is simple in principle — I want to be able to follow a young person's journey from the moment they register with us, through applications they make, interviews they attend, all the way to whether they actually found work. And ideally, even after they find work, I'd want to know — did they stay in that job? Did it last? Because a job that lasts three weeks isn't really a success story.

data.user_message:
Let me think about this in practical terms... When someone registers, that's the starting point obviously. Then when they apply for a job, that should be recorded. If an employer shows interest and calls them for an interview, that's an important moment — and honestly, right now we'd never even know that happened. Then whether they got offered the job, whether they accepted it, and then — and this is the part I really care about — whether they're still in that job after three months, after six months. Because that tells us whether we made a real difference or just a temporary one.

I'd also want to capture things like if they completed a training programme in the meantime, because sometimes a young person needs to build their skills before they're ready for work, and that journey matters too. The challenge is — how do we actually get this information? The employer might tell us they hired someone, but will they remember to tell us six months later if that person is still working? That's the part I'm not sure about.

The system shall track job seeker milestones, including registration, job applications, interviews, job offers, job acceptance, and employment status at 3 and 6 months, as well as completed training programs.
</REQ>

data.user_message:
I think it has to work from both sides — you can't rely on just one. The job seeker themselves should get reminders asking them to update their status — "did you find work? are you still employed?" — because they have the most direct interest in keeping their information current. But we also need the employer to confirm things, because a young person might tell us they got hired but give us wrong details, or they might forget to update us. So maybe when an employer marks a vacancy as filled, the system asks them to confirm who they hired from our platform.

And honestly, for the longer term follow-up — the three month, six month check-ins — I think we might need my regional staff to be involved too. Not for every single case, but for a sample at least. Because some of our young people, especially those with lower education levels or disabilities, might need a phone call rather than an automated message. They might not be checking an app regularly. So the platform should support that human follow-up as well, not try to replace it entirely.

<REQ type="functional" category="job_seeker_progress_tracking">
The system shall send reminders to job seekers to update their employment status and request employers to confirm hires, with support for regional staff to conduct follow-ups for long-term tracking.
</REQ>

data.user_message:
Honestly, that's something I haven't thought much about specifically... I suppose what I'd want is that the platform is simple enough that most people don't need documentation at all. Our job seekers are young people with mixed levels of education — some have university degrees, some barely finished secondary school. If they need to read a manual to figure out how to register, we've already failed. So whatever help is built in should be simple, visual, in plain language. And obviously it needs to be in Arabic — that's the first language of almost everyone using it.

For my staff, maybe some basic guidance on how to help job seekers who come into the office and need assistance using the platform. But again, if it's complicated enough that my staff need extensive training, that's a problem with the platform, not a documentation problem.

data.user_message:
I think on-screen prompts and simple step-by-step guidance as you go through the process makes more sense than a separate tutorial that people have to find and read. If someone is filling in their profile and they're not sure what to put in a field, there should be a small explanation right there, in plain Arabic. And for the really basic things — like how to upload a document or fill in a form — maybe short visual guides, not long text. Some of our younger users might be very comfortable with smartphones but have never used a formal registration system before.

But I want to come back to something — the platform also needs to work for people who come into our offices and have a staff member help them. Not everyone will register at home on their own. So the interface needs to be simple enough that my staff can sit with a young person and walk them through it together without it being a frustrating experience for either of them.

data.user_message:
I think the most important thing is that the staff interface is clean and straightforward — my regional officers are dedicated people but they are not technical, and I cannot have them spending an hour trying to figure out how to register one job seeker. The platform should guide the staff through the process just as much as it guides the job seeker. And if a young person comes in with paper certificates — which many of them will — the staff should be able to enter that information on their behalf without the whole thing becoming complicated.

I'd also want my staff to be able to see at a glance what's happening with the job seekers they've helped — did that person get matched to something, did they get an interview — so that when someone comes back to the office, the staff member doesn't have to start from scratch and ask "so who are you again?" That continuity matters a lot for building trust with young people who are already in a difficult situation.
data.assistant_message: How should the platform allow staff to track and manage the progress of job seekers they have assisted, ensuring continuity and trust?
